policy_statement: Close physical gaps and address challenging crossings within the existing and planned Tier 1 and Tier 2 trail system.
mechanism_description: The strategy focuses on closing gaps in the trail system and improving difficult crossings to make walking and biking trips easier and safer, supporting a shift away from car travel and lowering transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport infrastructure improvement → modal shift (↓ car VMT) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Cities build/upgrade active-transport networks (e.g., connected trails, protected crossings, safer intersections) to make walking and biking more convenient, safe, and continuous for everyday trips. (2) Mediating process: Improved perceived and actual safety + reduced travel time/effort increases active-mode share and decreases motorized trip frequency/length (lower VMT), reducing gasoline/diesel consumption. (3) Outcome: Lower tailpipe CO₂ (and some co-pollutants), yielding net reductions in transportation-sector GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions from fewer car trips/vehicle miles traveled due to a shift to walking and biking.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: infrastructure improvements induce modal shift away from cars, lowering fuel combustion and GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while such projects can have co-benefits (safety, health, congestion, some adaptation via heat reduction if paired with shade), the principal climate mechanism is emissions mitigation from reduced driving.  
3) Edge cases applied: if an “active transport” project were primarily for hazard evacuation routes or flood resilience, it could tilt toward Adaptation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets modal shift and emissions reduction, so Mitigation remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy framed as improving walking/biking infrastructure to shift trips from cars and reduce transport emissions shares the same primary mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., whether benefits come mainly from VMT reduction vs. electrification complementarity, or inclusion of demand management) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified only if the infrastructure is built mainly for non-mitigation purposes (e.g., recreation with negligible car-trip substitution, or resilience/evacuation), but the stated mechanism centers on modal shift and transport-emissions reduction, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.
key_indicators: "closing physical gaps"; "addressing challenging crossings"; "existing and planned Tier 1 and Tier 2 trail system"
co_benefits: Traffic safety (reduced crashes at crossings); public health benefits from increased physical activity; reduced local air pollution/noise; improved accessibility/mobility.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Strategy” language could imply a planning document, but the described actions (gap closure and crossing upgrades) are most consistent with physical capital improvements rather than a purely planning/strategy instrument.
